summary_sections.title: Lesson Overview
summary_sections.content_html:
Overview

This lesson covers the fundamental measures of center used to analyze and summarize data sets. You'll learn how to calculate and apply the mean, median, and mode, and understand when each is most appropriate.

summary_sections.title: Definitions and Concepts
summary_sections.content_html:
Key Definitions

What is the dimension of R^4?

The dimension of R^n is n, because the standard basis {e1, e2, ..., en} has exactly n vectors. For R^4, the standard basis has 4 vectors, so the dimension is 4.

What is the standard basis for R^3?

The standard basis for R^3 consists of the three unit vectors e1 = (1,0,0), e2 = (0,1,0), e3 = (0,0,1). These are linearly independent and span R^3, so they form a basis.

What is the dimension of the vector space of all 2x3 matrices?

The space of m x n matrices has dimension m * n. For 2x3 matrices, the dimension is 2 * 3 = 6. A basis consists of the 6 matrices with a single 1 in each possible position and zeros elsewhere.

What is the dimension of P_2, the space of polynomials of degree at most 2?

P_2 has the standard basis {1, x, x^2}, which contains 3 elements. Therefore dim(P_2) = 3. In general, P_n has dimension n + 1.

What is the dimension of the subspace W = {(x, y, z) in R^3 : x + y + z = 0}?

The equation x + y + z = 0 gives z = -x - y. So every vector in W has the form (x, y, -x-y) = x(1, 0, -1) + y(0, 1, -1). The vectors (1, 0, -1) and (0, 1, -1) are linearly independent and span W, forming a basis. Therefore dim(W) = 2.

What is the dimension of the trivial subspace {0}?

The trivial subspace {0} has dimension 0. Its basis is the empty set (which has 0 elements), since the empty set is vacuously linearly independent and spans {0}.

If V has dimension n and W is a subspace of V with dim(W) = n, what is W?

If W is a subspace of V and dim(W) = dim(V) = n, then W = V. This is because a basis for W consists of n linearly independent vectors in V, and n independent vectors in an n-dimensional space must span the entire space.

What is a basis for a vector space?

A basis for a vector space V is a set of vectors that is both linearly independent and spans V. Every vector in V can be written as a unique linear combination of the basis vectors.

What is the 'dimension' of a vector space?

The dimension of a vector space is defined as the number of vectors in any basis for the space. This is well-defined because all bases for a given vector space have the same number of elements.

What is meant by 'coordinates with respect to a basis'?

Given a basis B = {b1, ..., bn}, the coordinates of a vector v with respect to B are the unique scalars c1, ..., cn such that v = c1*b1 + c2*b2 + ... + cn*bn. The coordinate vector is [v]_B = (c1, c2, ..., cn).

What is the 'standard basis' for a vector space?

The standard basis for R^n is {e1, e2, ..., en} where ei is the vector with 1 in position i and 0 in all other positions. For polynomial spaces P_n, the standard basis is {1, x, x^2, ..., x^n}.

What is an 'ordered basis'?

An ordered basis is a basis in which the vectors are listed in a specific order. The order matters because the coordinate representation [v]_B depends on the ordering of the basis vectors. Swapping two basis vectors changes the coordinate vector.

What is a 'change of basis matrix'?

A change of basis matrix P converts coordinates expressed in one basis B to coordinates in another basis B'. If [v]_B are the coordinates of v in basis B, then [v]_{B'} = P * [v]_B gives the coordinates in basis B'.

What is the 'dimension formula' for subspaces (dim(W1 + W2))?

The dimension formula states dim(W1 + W2) = dim(W1) + dim(W2) - dim(W1 ∩ W2). This is analogous to the inclusion-exclusion principle for counting. It accounts for the overlap between the two subspaces.

A network engineer models network traffic using vectors in R^8, where each component represents bandwidth on a different link. She determines that only 3 of the 8 link measurements are independent (the others are determined by routing constraints). What is the dimension of the subspace of feasible traffic patterns?

If only 3 of the 8 measurements are independent and the rest are determined by linear constraints, then the subspace of feasible traffic patterns has dimension 3. A basis would consist of 3 traffic pattern vectors from which all others can be constructed.

A quantum computing researcher works with qubits, which live in a 2-dimensional complex vector space C^2. She uses the basis {|0>, |1>} (standard computational basis). She wants to switch to the Hadamard basis {|+>, |->} where |+> = (|0> + |1>)/sqrt(2) and |-> = (|0> - |1>)/sqrt(2). What is the dimension of the space in the new basis?

Dimension is an intrinsic property of the vector space, independent of the choice of basis. Since both the computational basis and the Hadamard basis have 2 vectors, the space is 2-dimensional regardless of which basis is used.

A structural engineer uses finite element analysis with 100 nodes, giving a model in R^100. She discovers that the stiffness matrix has rank 97. What is the dimension of the space of rigid body motions (null space)?

By the rank-nullity theorem, dim(null space) = n - rank = 100 - 97 = 3. This means the structure has 3 independent rigid body motions (translations and/or rotations that produce zero strain), which is physically meaningful for a 2D structure.

A data analyst has a dataset with 500 samples of 20 features each. She performs PCA and finds that the first 4 principal components capture 99% of the variance. She decides to project data onto a 4-dimensional subspace. What is the dimension of her reduced feature space?

The 4 principal components form a basis for the 4-dimensional subspace. After projection, each data point is represented by 4 coordinates (its components along each principal component), so the reduced feature space has dimension 4.

summary_sections.title: Real-World Applications
summary_sections.content_html:
Real-World Applications

Example: A photographer represents images as vectors in R^(1000000) (1 megapixel). She discovers that all her landscape photos lie in a 50-dimensional subspace. How many basis images does she need to reconstruct any landscape from her collection?

Since the landscape photos lie in a 50-dimensional subspace, any basis for that subspace has exactly 50 vectors. She needs 50 basis images, and any landscape from her collection can be reconstructed as a linear combination of these 50 images.
